I was in London last week and decided to see the exciting musical, Notre Dame de Paris.  Let's be honest, this may be the worst large-scale musical in the history of theater.  The show is so bad that I had to buy the recording.  The lyrics are both abysmal and repetitive, the music is uninteresting, and the performances are way over the top.  Everyone should buy this CD because this musical and this recording may be discussed forever in the annals of musical theater disasters.  Honestly, this score is so bad, it is actually fun to listen to.  " BELLE, There's a demon inside her who came from HELL.  And he turned my eyes from God, and oh, I FELL. "  " Torn Apart.  I am a man divided.  Torn Apart.  I want two women's love.  Two women want my love.  I don't know how to cut my heart in two. "  Enjoy!

An unexpected surprise -- for times when Parsifal won't do (eg cooking). Allers is most convincing and there's a sense of fun that is appealing to these ears. Granted that, as a night at the opera, this wouldn't do (Fledermaus is way too much to accept) but as a recording this is to be recommended.

The complete organ works on a 2 cd box as played by the fine Dutch organist Leo van Doeselaar (and pupil of André Isoir) were recorded around 1984 in excellent sound.  Using two local organs (Lohman 1828 and Mueller 1724), it is impossible to believe they could be bettered.  Newer recordings of this repertoire could equal this mastery,  but the unique voice of Wilhelm Friedemann is relayed here in unsurpassable form.

This is not salon music.  In Russian song and poetry, the moment is celebrated, with vehemence or melancholy. The moment of existence is treasured. The typically superb technique of Gedda (unbelievable ascents into head voice) is allied to a quite piercing emotional response that it is evident the tenor felt personally. One of the greatest recordings of its kind, and if you have any serious interest in world song, Russian art and music, or great singing, the disc is essential. Fear not the opinions of the misled condescending.

A beautiful, confident sound with a tad too much vibrato -- the tones are loosening in pitch (she's doubtlessly pushing for a bigger sound).  However, it cannot be mistaken for a profound interpretation such as any of Janet Baker's, where one never is conscious of the sound, the mechanics of singing or breath-control, or verbal emphasis.  One is in the still presence of the composer and his art.  Thus does the veil fall as Dame Janet sings.

This is a very dark voice with a chalky top. The  imperfect technique (scooping to painful high notes, discolored vowels, wavering sustained notes) cruelly expose the fact that this is nothing like the great soprano lieder singers of the past like Ameling or today, like Christine Schafer. More suited to the Obradors or Turina as long as you don’t think of Caballé or Verrett.

This gem of a recording (1987) features a superb program of long forgotten composers (except for Dowland) who were active in service to Christian IV who reigned over Denmark and Norway from 1588 to the Peace of Westphalia (1648), the longest in Danish history. The artists include the well known Jakob Lindberg (lute), the Dowland Consort of viols (UK) and the six member Royal Danish Brass ensemble. The music, performances and sound are exemplary.  The contrast between the soloist, the consort of viols and the brilliant brass group is integral to the success of this very fine disc.  Aficionados of early seventeenth century music should not hesitate.

If you are tired of practicing scales and modes with no idea for how they are useful, pick up this CD and try out your improv skills over some really nice sounding backups. All the components of a band are there (rhythm, bass and drums) except for lead guitar and vocals.
The wide variety of keys and tempos is really great, but I do have to say that a lot of the tracks on the disc aren't as bluesy as I would have liked, and some are very close to being classic rock in sound. That is great, but I had really hoped to have some nice 12 bar to play in front of (there are enough of those to keep me happy).
At the price, this should keep you entertained with a fun way to practice your scales in a non-linear fashion. I had a guitar teacher that used to do this same thing and we would play for 10 minutes a lesson, I think it really helped me to visualize the scales on the fretboard, which made my playing much more fluid.

Positives: superb sound, conscientious singing by a light, soprano-ish mezzo, idiomatic French and fine styling. Weaknesses: range of voice limited (so weak and colorless if not quite non-existent lower register, key to so many essential phrases such as 'sur la mer'), lack of dramatic poignancy, despite often slower-than-normal tempi, that distinguishes the truly unforgettable recordings (Baker, Crespin, etc), lack of vocal coloration emphasizes the exceptional beautiful but ultimately rather characterless voice.

'Ma belle amie est morte; je pleurerai toujours'....I don't quite believe the singer.

In the Lyric Pieces sweepstakes, this pianist and series (of which this is volume II) doesn't place. Pleasant enough on its own terms, this disc cannot be recommended in view of the strong competition. Too often, the fast pieces are undetailed and hearty in a 'peasant' sense, and the melancholy pieces merely slow. This sound is cavernous and well below standard for 1991.

There are many worthy single disc compilations as contenders for the true Grieg spirit (Gilels, Andsnes, Pletnev, but above all, Richter, from his final years). For the same opuses as played here, Peter Katin is exquisite. In fact, his three disc traversalof the complete Lyric Pieces have not been bettered despite much recent (and very shiny) competition.

Unlike some studio recordings, this is flesh-and-blood, with an astonishing Caballe and a wonderful tenor Fabiano Labo, in excellent sound considering it's an older pirate.  While Caballe was recognized as one of the greatest voices of the post-war era, it's sad to contemplate that such a remarkable and beautiful instrument such as Labo's has vanished from memory.  As a reality check, consider the most recent Piratas as the Met -- do not accept imitations, this is the genuine thing: great voices and singing.

Unidiomatic, lethargic and not recommended -- obviously most of the people reading the reviews look for their own pov to be corroborated, rather than serious criticism. As a result, the detailed review by the Man in a Hathaway is dissed and anodyne treacle about this being a great recording is widely applauded. Welcome to democracy in action where the lowest common denominator prevails.

In any event, this is really an embarrassing disc (perhaps Arrau did better later) and if you think this is great, then you should simply ignore the readings of selected etudes by the likes of Richter and Horowitz and the best complete sets, those by Ohlsson and Ashkenazy. If you like distorted accents, lumpen phrasing, clumsy technique -- you will certainly find many of those grotesqueries here. I think it very likely, based on the overwhelming evidence left behind by Chopin's contemporaries, that the composer would have been startled -- to say the least -- by this elephantine effort.

This artist has followed a consistent downwards spiral since her youth thought perhaps this disc captures her absolute nadir (the Bach is played like Prokofiev, the Chopin is complete tatters). From the poetry of her first recitals for DG in the 70s, we have now come to dubious technical skills, complete rhythmic breakdown, and grotesque ‘expressivity’. Certainly the worst c# minor Scherzo in the catalogue, completely awash in over pedaling, and crass insensitivity to the extraordinary central ‘organ’ section. Really unlistenable except perhaps the Prokofiev, but then the imprecision of her fingers doesn’t compensate for her energy. Besides, so many others from Richter on have made music of Prokofiev instead of just noise. I had been warned, but I was curious.  The disc now moves on to the local thrift.

A fourth star is awarded for by the presence of two rather obscure French composers, Maurice Delage and Maurice Joubert.  The raison d’être of the disc is to showcase the mélodie specialist Felicity Lott who sings well on the whole. However, the opening piece, the Delage “Quatre poèmes hindous” is tonally monochromatic and a bit strident under pressure, not to be compared to an early recording by her compatriot Dame Janet Baker who captures both the rapture and mystery of these works which must have been very striking at the time of composition (1912).  It’s good to have the Joubert cycle “Saisir” on disc and Mme Lott sings well. Competition is stiff in the Chausson and this interpretation is solid but once again not ideally smooth tonally.

This recording has almost everything: a superb ensemble complemented by fine soloists, well-recorded sound and good production values (and a bonus, excellent booklet essay -- did the conductor ever read this?). Yet it is serious disappointment which reveals the limitations of Christie as he strays further afield of the courtly French baroque style with its euphonious if artificial glaze.

In place of true understanding and sympathy for this music, Christie substitutes his wind-up version of 'authenticity', with absurdly fast tempi (venture no further than the opening overture, where it sounds like the musicians have been spooked by a bolt of lightning). From this mechanical solution, no expressive potential can be unlocked. Layered on top is a clotted vocal texture (Christie adds additional voices to the 'choral' sections, a real backfire) which destroys the transparency of this perfect watercolor. A canvas with thick impasto is all that remains. In short, Christie's direction is too pompous, gauche and unfeeling to be matched to this masterpiece.

Gardiner and King are to be strongly preferred, and even van Asch's single disc on Naxos, a 'barebones' production (which never sounds rushed) captures the spirit of this pastoral composition in a far more convincing realization.

A rather understated performance (even by traditional English oratorio standards) --- accurate and dutiful enough, but having much less impact than either the McCreesh or Gardiner. Moreover, the double casting of the four  soprano roles is cheesy.  Little wonder that the second-tier choral conductor Reuss is headed to Estonia for his next permanent appointment (this is not a slur on Estonia).

This moving Christmas oratorio of the 23-year old composer is very well served by this forty-year old reading, captured in an airy acoustic with superb sound. The five vocal soloists (including the well-known Edith Wiens and Kurt Widmer) are captured in a forward but not unpleasant way, and they sing like angels. The tenor aria (Friedrich Melzer) is unusually poignant — the distribution of the numbers is perfectly balanced (solo arias for soprano and tenor, a duet for soprano and bass, a trio that adds tenor, a quartet, and a quintet (adding a second soprano) — as is the entire effort. I’m not familiar with other recordings of this work, but I cannot conceive of a more effective reading of this early masterpiece.

This DVD would more aptly be titled "Not Much Then, and Way Too Much Now."  Tommy Caldwell would turn over in his grave if he saw this slick massacre of all those beautiful old MTB tunes.  How dare they play  This Ol' Cowboy, such an earthy, heart felt song, in such a disco manner  and even add a bunch of notes to the main riff.  But the big disappointment  was they only played ONE song showing the original members from the old  days (Can't You See).  That Band always was, and always will be for me, not  about Doug Gray or that flute, but Toy and Tommy Caldwell standing up there  like honorable old cowboys, playing with their thumbs (literally) and  jamming with a sound they created and nobody else has ever imitated.  It is  a crying shame that they are both gone, what wonderful minstrels they were.

Instead of telling you how much I know about the Beatles and having you think of me as a pompous record geek, I am going to tell you two things about this album:
1. You will know every track on this album and will likely be able to sing along with it right out of the wrapper.
2. There are seminal pieces of Beattle-mania that are not included on this album because they were not number one hits. You can fault EMI for that if you want, but they had to draw the line somewhere, and when a band has enough #1's to fill a whole CD, that is probably a good demarcation point.
In my mind, the first comment was more important than the second, enough for me to give it 4 stars. Buy it and enjoy!

This represents Mozart from a 'grand opera' approach. Although, indeed, Idomeneo as an entirety is the grandest of the master's operas, Levine's approach is heavy and pumped up -- in the wrong way. As a result, it feels here much longer than the chronological duration [even though this is a far from inclusive edition of the various possible performing editions of Idomeneo]. Add to that the fact that the well-known singers are generally far from ideal, with all leads except perhaps the Ilia not evincing much of the chiaroscuro that is embedded in Mozart's extraordinary re-creation of Gluckian opera seria.

This opera still awaits a completely satisfactory recording but all three of Colin Davis's reading are more rewarding. I have a fondness for his first, with a fresh-voiced Jessye Norman as Idamante -- Davis would soon cast her as the Countess in her first major complete studio opera -- and Gedda in superb form. The Gardiner is spirited in the right way, with Harnoncourt in the wrong way, and the recent Jacobs is typically imaginative -- all three point up the lead weight in Levine's rendition.

This is a fine selection of numbers (“highlights”) from this early work of Verdi which, in some critical estimations, the composer never excelled. As he drew from one of the darkest of Shakespeare’s works, with its emphatic incorporation of supernatural elements, Verdi responded with inspiration to the violent and ghoulish aspects of this sanguinary work. That he continued to think about the work that he believed in despite its mixed reception is evidenced by his 1861 revision with insertions more typical of his middle period.

Between the Preludio and the Finale, this disc presents a reasonable overview of the drama with fine conducting and singing in almost every aspect. In addition to the celebrated opening Witches’ scene, there is the famous choral lament “Patria oppressa”, the Chorus of Assassins, and the eerie Witches’ Spell. The two most powerful arias of Lady, Banquo’s aria, Macduff’s poignant lament to his two sons, and, finally, Macbeth’s last act aria where he reveals both a certain madness but a moving tragic grandeur — all are included.

The disc’s relatively short timings reflect its origin on LP but the opportunity to hear these excerpts (the two disc complete opera is rare and extremely costly) featuring Sass, Cappuccilli, Christoff (no less), and the fine tenor Peter Kélen, is truly fortunate with Hungaraton’s licensing to Laserlight.

This inexpensive reissue, without libretto but including decent notes, is very welcome and establishes that wonderful Handel singers are not exclusive to our own times.  Rene Jacobs is a known quantity as an alto, and he is at least the equal of the current crop (Daniels, etc); Isabelle Poulenard is even-toned and lovely, as is the agile and stylish Sophie Boulin, bright-voiced but never shallow sounding, as can be too easily encountered.  Everything about this recording reflects the genius of the composer and these gifted modern interpreters.  Lack of drama -- well, that is a personal taste, but I find the sheer musicality is absorbing and satisfying.

That Dame Janet was born to sing Wolf was very clear despite little recorded evidence (afaik, only four 'geistliche' songs from Wolf's Spanisches Liederbuch for EMI). Thus, this issue now fills an important vacuum in the discography of this greatest of lieder singers. She is in superlative voice and, as is almost always the case with this artist, is an unforgettable interpreter of these lieder which, in many ways, are the pinnacle of the German art song. This is a required acquisiton for serious lieder collectors; I would also advise listening to the release of Schoenberg lieder by Dame Janet on the European Ponto label.

Not to duplicate the previously posted very helpful review, this disc is indeed outstanding. That this long and subtle Wolf 'cycle' could be programmed in its entirety at Aldeburgh in 1976 is a tribute to the sophisticated and critical tastes of English audiences during that period. An attractively typeset German text and English translation is easily downloaded.

Having heard Rattle conduct this symphony at Disney Hall with the LAPhil, this recording seems an accurate facsimile of the overall experience. It’s what I’d call a technical rendition, and a superb one at that, but it’s nonetheless that of an outsider looking in at something he understands the parts of, but not the whole. Considering the number of choices, particularly historical or quasi-historical, it’s not a preferred choice (imho) although arguably it may convince a neophyte that this composer warrants further investigation.

Symphonies don’t have to have four movements, despite the composer’s sketches. The three movement work stands on its exalted pedestal, as does Schubert’s Unfinished. For this listener, the perfection of that work far exceeds Schubert’s complete C Major which I can no longer survive.

Warning -- the cover of the box shows a Lohengrin that is not included in the actual set -- there were prior graphics that showed that this was the Steber Elsa. It's to be expected that a 12 disc box would indeed feature four operas (4x3). If you are interested in this comparatively rare Lohengrin, as I was, do not expect it to be here.

Just to add a couple of points to the extensive prior review (which seems quite withholding in generosity) -- (1) this early, pastoral-flavored music has its own exquisite pleasure, pasticcio not withstanding (after all, the Met can mount their forgettable Enchanted Island this year based on ripping off music from Handel, Rameau, Vivaldi, Bob Dylan et al -- at least Handel arranged this largely himself); (2) the singers are, to a person, consummately skilled and possessed of beautiful timbres (Katalin Farkas is quite special) and, morever, McGegan scales his interpretation very naturally; (3) in this day and age where Handel is ripe for modernized (read Eurotrash) stagings and their concomitant overripe voices (eg Fleming), this is a tremendous relief to turn to this 80s recording with its sincerity and commercial-free drama.  We are not so fortunate today.  Despite claims that we live in a golden age of Handel singing, I'm afraid that is a mere self-serving statement by opera company managements, singers and their agents, and the large recording companies. This Capella Savaria gem is just the antidote to modern musical hype.

But we don't appreciate him trying to pawn off a greatest hits album on his devoted fans. At the very least, his cover of "Sussudio" -- otherwise only available on a ... German import -- could have been included.

Despite a brilliant high register where this voice can soar unimpeded, the lower range sounds tarnished and abraded.  The technique is serviceable, as are the middle-of-the-road interpretations.  It may be likely that hearing this legendary singer in the house (or at least in live recordings) would supply the vividness that these readings lack, and the graininess of the timbre would be less noticeable as heard in the theater. I wouldn't short-list any of the tracks here as providing a definitive interpretation of the aria in question, but in toto, this is a valuable record of a famous soprano in characteristic form.

Regardless of how good a selection of Hahn this is, or how well recorded this disc is, it remains one to which I will not often return. The music itself is rarely rises about its 3/5 quality level, and the singing seldom rises above 4/5. Though 'friends', Poulenc's endowment far excelled Hahn's gifts. Actually, many songs are, in fact, forgettable.
Many friends love Cradle of Filth. I've tried to get into them to make polite conversation. It just doesn't work. Not really sure why. Maybe if they sang about wombats?

"Deluxe 180 gram vinyl re-issues."
Ahem.

The record jacket is some of the cheapest, flimsiest material I've seen used for a jacket. It's matte, thin, and has pretty poor printing on it. The sleeve is almost as thick as the jacket (!)

Record sleeve is almost as thick as the record jacket. The printing is marginally better, but it's certainly nothing to show off. Furthermore, it's made from rough cardboard, so it scuffed over 25% of the record before it was even opened.

The record itself is the proper weight, and has clean edges. However, there is a bunch of residue from the manufacturing process on it, not to mention the aforementioned scuffs over 1/4 of the record from said bad sleeve.

I will try to give this one a wet clean (Groovewasher) and see if it's salvageable, but I'm doubtful.

Whoever designed the packaging should be ashamed of themselves. This is seriously some of the chintziest album packaging I have ever seen. Even plain paper jackets & sleeves from the 80s are better, as they don't immediately destroy the album. Surfer Rosa is a fantastic album and deserves a proper repressing, with artwork that will keep the media safe. Shame on you!

I got this because I had read an article about how Richard Thompson was one of the great unknown artists out there and I was intrigued by the idea of picking the best Popular Music from a 1000 years back, but it has not really panned out.

I like classical music and folk music and I was hoping to get a bit of a music lesson, but I came away without knowing why he picked these particular songs and what was important about them. They're not always even played in chronological order, so there is no sense of connection from one to the other. In some of the more recent music, I recognized many of them, but again did not know why he thought they were important. I was left with the unfortunate suspicion that he was able to get the copyright use more cheaply than for other more important work.

He is also suppose to be a virtuosic guitar player. I'm no expert, but it certainly does not come out in these songs.

Some of the songs are interesting, but it did not seem worth the money in the end. This was probably not the best introduction to Richard Thompson, but I'm not sure I'll be trying any of his other albums.

Sinopoli is decent, the principal singers lacking in understanding and charm (Dessay is the exception), quite put into the shade by the so-called secondary characters [Dohmen, the Genzes,etc].  Bright, clean sound --- and all meaningless when the phrasing is lifeless and uncomprehending.  Voigt's big aria is, indeed, 'Totenreich'  --- lugubrious, rhythmically flaccid, and totally graceless.  Yes, the notes ping, but so do those of a synthesizer.  Gramophone is spot on to call this voice 'stainless steel'.  For those who are unconvinced by this Strauss opera, this will provide ample reinforcement.  For those who believe in Ariadne, this recording will test their faith.  In a word:  stillborn.

With all due respect to the other review here, which is excellent and perceptive, from a pianist's point of view, the liner notes by the peerless Piero Rattalino are treasurable (he calls what I agree is a not convincing Cziffra rendition of the Chopin études as 'corpulent like creatures of El Greco repainted by Botero'). The Waldstein glissando problem is well-known, as is the Schnabel amendation set forth in his famous edition of the 32. The glissando is clearly not playable on the modern piano, and listening to a recording may not resolve the question of which slight-of-hand is being used.

But the defense of Cziffra's restraint and musicality (at least from this 1963 Lugano recital) is completely justified. There is no playing to the gallery. The Chopin 'Heroic' polonaise is indeed most discreet (perhaps slightly undercharacterized in comparison with today's norms) but not unsatisfyingly so. The Waldstein is very fine, shorn of the accretions of rhetoric that have accumulated through the decades. Almost pristine -- fascinating, agile, perfectly articulated and transparent technique. Rattalino's description of Cziffra's Carnaval is a bullseye -- and perceptive on the topic of why some us are always a little disappointed with the final 'Davidsbündler' section.

A truly special recording (by RSI) which restores some breadth to this extraordinary artist's documented oeuvre.
Not as expected

Even in a golden age of Chopinists, Malcuzynski was the glorious star of EMI and it is good to have just these 2 discs of these recordings from the 60s, in excellent sound. His Waltzes imho are superior to the lauded Lipatti and Rubinstein recordings -- instead of trivializing these seminal works with excessive speed and clipped, weightless tones, Malcuzynski produces true richness of touch, suppleness of phrasing and rubato, and exquisitely judged tonal shadings that bring out the underlying melancholy of Chopin's dance music. The pianist truly vindicates Schumann who claimed that if these waltzes were to be danced to, they would have to be danced by countesses. The mazurkas, too, are superior to the praised Rubinstein version(s), in their greater tautness of line, rhythmic verve, and tonal beauty.

The concerto and the sonata (op 58) are equally fine -- even flawless. Certainly Malcuzynski's sonatas were far superior to the Rubinstein versions (which garnered lukewarm reviews even at the time). Unfortunately, the second sonata (op 35) is not included, which has a stunning coda to the first movement -- far more dynamic than the tame Rubinstein (I owned both, but you know which one had true passion for me).

Where are Malcuzynski's scherzi, ballades, the even more wonderful polonaises and his selection of nocturnes? I suppose we must be grateful for little favors. His memory endures.

The Shaggs - the enigma, the legacy, the impossible musical feats. It's fairly hard to characterize or describe them.
This is an album for someone who wants to hear sounds that they'll never encounter before. For someone who wants to experience a contemporary audio atavism. For someone who dares travel to the edge of known music.
I actually enjoy listening to their music - there's a strange exotic naivete to it that is refreshingly challenging.

This rendition captures every aspect of this elusive, beautiful and sincere work of art. While there are other well-vocalized versions, this is the supreme recording of Lakme as it uncovers the melancholy poetry of the remarkable setting, the plot, and the characters. Unequivocal salutes to Dessay at her absolute peak in vocalism and interpretation and Kunde who touches the heart through the wonderful timbre of voice and expressive simplicity.

Disappointing evidence of the decline in orchestral control and interpretive insight of Giulini in the sunset of his wonderful career. There is lack of tautness, focus, and finish in execution which, alone, is enough to relegate this to the second tier (at best) of Bruckner 7s. Even more seriously, the inconsistent pulse and rhythmic flabbiness in evidence in every movement -- in every section of every movement in fact -- disqualifies this as a collector's choice. After all, if you can't keep a single consistent beat in the first exposition of key theme or motive, just how can you command the vast architecture of Bruckner's symphonic vision? If you can't remember your original tempo, how do expect a listener to grasp the recapitulation?

There are perhaps a dozen interpretations of the seventh which are preferable to this, above all, the versions of Wand whose vision sees to the molten core of our planet -- the immutable center of Bruckner. Another example would be the little-known but unforgettable reading of von Matacic and the Czech Philharmonic -- not the most perfect execution perhaps, but irrelevant in view of the comprehension of the Bruckner cosmos.

As a revenue generator for the major labels of the time (the usual suspects), it’s a truly ludicrous idea to acquire this as a ginormous box set. Who would buy this? The needless duplication of repertoire, the often aberrant choices (André Previn? Maria Yudina?) and glaringly omissions (Annie Fischer), skimpy documentation and barely passable notes simply add up to a single message to this vanity project: avoid.

Lang Lang's expert proficiency and artistic feeling provides a powerful experience. Intense at times, gentle and reflective at others - it's not "background music". Western style compositions with Asian melodies and theme. Unique and very enjoyable.

Having recordings of these quartets by multiple groups, the finest of which is doubtless the intégrale of the Hagens, this disc by the Jerusalem is the first since then that is their equal. Technical address and finesse, stylistically redoubtable, and deep understanding of the complex simplicity of these scores -- all suggest the immediate need for more Jerusalems in Mozart. Superlative recording of pellucid transparency.

Extremely moving performance by Miriam Gauci, with her sensitivity and extraordinarily beautiful sound (if you like the uneven vocal textures of late Sutherland or Scotto, look elsewhere), well-seconded by lesser known but fine singers such as Lucienne van Deyck (Principessa), and wonderfully and idiomatically conducted by Rahbari (as usual) -- this ranks at the top of available recordings alongside of Ricciarelli (in rarely caught pristine voice).  It’s far preferable to the efforts of the aging divas mentioned above and far ahead of the recent Andris release whose soprano whose voice is vulnerable in the wrong way.  The recorded sound is excellent with slightly resonant space around the voices suggestive of the cloisters.

It is difficult to imagine a better introduction to meditative music from the late medieval period than this disc which bears witness to the extremely high standards of British performers in music of this period -- some 30+ years ago.  Cantores, an Exeter University group under David Allinson, performs 79 minutes of masterpieces that could hardly be better chosen: two Stabat Maters (Josquin, Lassus), a gem by Jean Mouton, the inspiring In Media Vita of John Sheppard, and, last, but not least, one of the many unforgettable short motets of Gombert: Lugebat David Absalon, the lament of King David for his son.  An irresistible cornerstone for a collector, new or otherwise, to this music which exists outside time.

Very disappointed in this. Have always like Michael Feinstein's voice and George Shearing's piano but really expected more. The atmosphere is like sitting in a piano bar lounge which should be great for old songs but the composition of the lyrics and the way Feinstein is singing them is almost depressing. Very little voice inflection to the point when almost every song sounds the same.

The two prior reviews are splendid and I add just add one thought to them. In these last orchestral works of d’Indy there is wonderfully evocative quality of time and place that is the essence of the contemporaneous French musical environment.  If you are familiar with the Vaughn Williams/Delius works of the period, this is worthy area to explore. To my mind, there is less reliance on nostalgia (via the modal harmonies of the English composers) and finer sensitivity to nature approached through wonderfully transparent orchestration of which, of course, d’Indy was a master and teacher (and textbook author). Prêtre is superb, the orchestra fine, and recording more than adequate.

Though I am unfamiliar with its competitors, this recording can hardly be bettered, in my mind, in terms of soloists (including well known singers such as the sweet voiced Hans Peter Blochwitz and Cornelius Hauptmann), choir and pacing (Dieter Kurtz). In addition, the aural capture is very fine.

Schütz’s spanned turbulent years in the seventeenth century (Thirty Years War ended 1648) and was centered on Dresden where he was active in predecessors to the Staatskapelle Dresden. More significantly he ventured to Italy where he was trained by Gabrieli (whom Schütz referred to as his master) and brought back his discoveries of Italian polyphony and antiphony to Germany. He wrote the Matthaüs Passion in his 81st year (he lived to 87) and it reflects not only the reduced musical resources available after the destructive wars, but his own development of an austere style punctuated with dissonance. Together with Buxtehude, he was a formative influence on J S Bach. Amateur and church choirs have long depended on his compositions including his short, poignant motets.

Not a disc for everyone, but if drawn to the contemplative music of this period, it is highly recommended.

Music (court, chapel, popular), history and politics (Carlos V - the 'first and last great Emperor of Europe', as Savall writes), culture and religion --- all intersect in this disc and its documentation which make it essential for all serious music lovers as well as cultural historians of all stripes.  It is impossible to conceive a superior sound picture of the 16th century and one is left with the conclusion that this period was the absolute zenith of European civilization. There is also an extremely fine disc by Alexander Blachly and Pomerium built around the same theme as this (Carolus Maximus) which is fully worthy of acquisition as well; it features perhaps less variety than this, but is an equally superb recording.
This remains the apogee of English song recitals. The ineffable voice and interpretation of Dame Janet are timeless. An imperative purchase for anyone interested in 20th century song.

Live recordings are unsurpassed when they enshrine a superb moment in the theater -- but are painful when they capture a low quality experience such as this. Yes, the young Pavarotti is worth listening to, and Molinari-Pradelli is typically solid; Cappucilli is fine, too. The distaff side is beyond hope. The Alisa is sung by a 'voce brutta' but yields to the supreme witch sounds of Scotto who violates every dictum of good singing -- lunging at notes both high and low, portamenti inducing seasickness, and a kind of italicized screaming which would have delighted the Second Viennese School. It's amazing that no one suggested she undertake Lulu forthwith.

Moments of expressiveness for which Scotto became identified simply cannot obscure the damage she inflicts on this heroine. In fact, this must be the worst she sang ever -- her later studio Butterfly is under far more control. Could there be a weaker Lucia in the catalogue?
I didn't realize there were vocals on this album.  I don't like my tango smeared with vocals but I should have read more about it I guess.

There are vintage recordings, despite sound, whose distinction is so clear that they mandate acquisition. This isn’t one of them. The sound in a shoebox can’t conceal the routine here — piano and orchestra. With likely 30 or more alternatives (from the usual suspects), it would be a mistake to think this is remotely capable of bringing Mozart’s inspiration to life. From the same era, Annie Fischer is vastly preferable.

This disc was a surprise, as their boxed Mozart cycle and many assorted Beethoven discs on DG are in my collection and I think very, very highly of them. I recently saw one of their live Mozart performances on DVD which was absolutely riveting. It's difficult to conceive that this seasoned quartet (near 30 years as the 3 Hagen members plus a fourth) could improve on their reference recordings.

But they have in every respect that I can hear. With unfaltering technical address, these readings now have a kind of un-self-conscious freedom that I can only describe as 'plasticity'. What I found previously 'unexcelled' has now become peerless.

The Myrios sound is even finer than DG as the quartet is set in a very wide and open soundstage, without any loss of aural precision.

Although I'm infrequently motivated to pull out Parsifal to listen to it in its entirety, the two times I've seen I seen it on stage have been unexpectedly magical. Of my several recordings, this is the one I pull out the least often. Upon rehearing it recently, I can now say why. There is no magic, no spiritual plane here (for me). Instead of a flowing quality to the fastish tempi, I heard boredom. Instead of inwardness, I hear bluntness without any breathing or phrasing. Boulez's lack of musicality, light and shade, and moments of illumination -- the oft criticized Solti reaches much farther into time and space than this lumpen rendition, as does Kna in less vivid sound in 1962.  I shall not have time to return here again.

Sass created a sensation in the operatic world of the late 70s-80s with her lustrous, evenly produced voice with dark, spinto-like color and power. Sadly, like many a singer dubbed the “next Callas” (eg Suliotis), she was called, and accepted, to sing a wide range of dramatic roles (Tosca, Manon Lescaut) which led to vocal deterioration, and thus a shortened career. She, however, left several memorable studio assumptions of which her Lady Macbeth is legendary and very difficult to find. She maintains an active career today as a vocal coach in her native Hungary, and has written an admired autobiography. In several senses, the career she might had is the one pursued by her compatriot Julia Varady, who sang Sieglinde (Sass had her eye on this role, but she sang no staged Wagner). However there is no question in my mind that Sass’s voice had a unique beauty, one of the great sounds of her generation.

To hear what the excitement was about, this recital of Mozart and Wagner, one of her first, is revelatory. The tonal splendor of her voice is matched a technique that masters the transparent and agile demands of Mozart in his two most important orchestra/piano concert arias, and Elettra’s last act aria from Idomeneo. The Wesendonck lieder are among the finest in the catalogue, and the conducting by the well-known Ervin Lukacs is very fine. All in all, a disc that provides increasing pleasure after multiple auditions.

Levit hardly plumbs the depth of these exacting visionary works. In fact, these bland, smoothed readings are doubtless in the lower half of available alternatives. These are literal interpretations in the Pollini mold but without the Italian’s technical poise, not to speak of the stratospheric peaks of Schnabel, Richter or Annie Fischer.

In a comparison of Op 111 against his contemporary Simone Dinnerstein (live no less, in Berlin), she engages in a titanic struggle with the black and white notes of Beethoven’s peerless work. Levit passes his final year conservatory examination.

My review title can have two meanings.  Perhaps I just don't get this release.  My recommendation would also be...just don't get it.  If you enjoy hearing Natalie's voice, that's one thing.  By all means, go ahead and buy this.  But, to me, I just can't stomach this one.  I tried.  Gosh I tried.  I love Natalie's music and she MAY have been an influence in the naming of one of my children.  But I just find the subject matter too nonsensical and random. Underneath the execrable sound, there are two outstanding performances. Price and Obratzova cut through the murk and on that basis alone this recording would justify listening to.

The mishmash version that Abbado uses has only one serious fault: the bleating of monks after the final aria of Elisabetta. That postpones the enigmatic (to put it politely) ending of the opera, which needs to happen fast and furious so no one can pull an emperor's clothing by asking "Just what happened there?"
Not great.  I expected much more from the Piano Guys.

My friend is in love with Alejandro Fernandez, and asked for only one thing when I asked what she wanted for Christmas: his CD/DVD combo.
I thought I was being smart when I purchased this item: I checked out all the sellers on Amazon, found one with shipping honoured by Amazon, with great customer reviews, with reviews that were Amazon verified, a seller rating above 95%, and an item that I knew would be used but was described as "like new".

I am disappointed in this seller, and embarrassed at the item that arrived.
First off, the CD jewel case arrived with scratches...not a few cosmetics scratches which would have been fine, but scratches that were deep and running through the case - an indicator that an item is more than "like new".
Secondly, the audio CD doesn't play properly. It skips to the point where the music is not enjoyable.

As I said, DISAPPOINTED. As a seller of used items, there needs to be quality control - didn't anyone check the CD case, or the audio CD before shipping?
Now I have to find another seller, and buy this gift again and hope it arrives in time for New Year...because it's not cost effective to pay for return shipping and demand a refund.

I'm giving this item 2 stars because at least the DVD works, and I can buy her the songs on iTunes until the replacement arrives.

Very convincing in all aspects. Clearly this music is Franck-influenced and not first-rate, but it's certainly worth hearing as an exemplar of its period, and the sincerity of the composer's inspiration. A wonderful alternative to such over-exposed works such as the Deutsches Requiem.

Taut, expressive, and soundwise even tending to the astringent -- these are treasurable recordings from the only complete Haydn cycle on period instruments. Thankfully, the final volume (#4) was committed to disc and released after the death of the original executive producer of the series, Michel Bernstein.

This is essentially a central European interpretation which does not intend to dazzle with elegance or speed -- some have called it rustic. I think it's recognizably Papa. The Mosaiques are suaver, have plusher acoutics, and tend slightly to first violin dominance. I have their incomplete series as well, but the Festetics are gloriously different but equally cogent.

The Arcana sets are magnificently produced with beautifully printed, color-contrasted bifold (ie tripartite) folders and separate notes. Their thoughtfulness and aesthetics is a salute to this incomparable composer and the splendor of his quartet cycle. Brings to mind the equally wonderful (but alas, incomplete) Hogwood cycle for L'Oiseau-Lyre. I think Papa would be very pleased.

Concurring with the other reviews, this is indeed splendid in all respects. I add that in the part of one of the Angels [Engeln] is Andreas Scholl in one of his first recordings; the singer would later become one of the most important ‘haute contres’ of baroque music.  Reminds me a bit of hearing the earliest Janet Baker recordings — you could sense the nascence of a great musician.

Compared to the earlier DG disc in the same repertoire, and evaluated on an absolute standard of vocalism and style, this disc marks a serious decline in Terfel's voice -- frayed, pale lower notes, and lunging high notes (yikes, sad to say!)  For artistry in the same repertoire, looks elsewhere.  Terfel fans, beware...the glory days are over.  It's all quite tired -- he needs a serious break to nurture his voice and enthusiasm.

When played, even on the replacement, I noticed imperfections on the vinyl record itself, and a big chip on the side of the record. I have to admit that the second record was just thrown in the box and poorly shipped and delivered with the box wide open, but two discs with scratches and imperfections doesn't lead to a good sign.

The ideal, minimalist recording for pianists learning these works. The notes are there and obeisance to the tempo and dynamic marks of the composer.

However, the fusion of technical requirements and musical substance (as Frederic Chiu points out in his notes to his own fine recording of Opus 10, Chopin's pathbreaking innovation was promptly followed by Liszt whose first and revised version of his own Concert Études bears witness to having heard Opus 10) is barely discernible in this barebones rendition. With so many other fine versions available, this cannot be recommended.

Bookended by ten familiar but peerless Wolf lieder, this fine, dark-voiced mezzo programs songs of two Swiss composers, Hans Huber (1852-1921) and Rudolf Moser (1892-1960) -- creating a special and unusual disc which also benefits from pianist Manuel Baertsch. For cognoscenti of German lieder, this specialist recital is truly a treasure. Bravi to the wonderful performers and the Discover label that was courageous to record this 1994 recital.

Indispensable contribution to the legacies of one violinist and three composers. Taut, thrilling and fully engaged in the spirit of the works and times.  The sound is quite wonderful even in the earliest capture (the Khachaturian in 1940).
Fantastic selections, performed by nice voices, not an accolade- just a nice intro- there is a reason that the great opera singers are 'great'

Loved hearing this originally on the radio. Bought it hoping and expecting to love it. Volume was a real problem and it wasn't nuanced at all. Had to return it. Don't buy this. Problems all along the line, and had to return it.

I'm glad to cast a belated vote of unanimity with the other astute reviewers here! Having only just bought this disc -- to add to the 'essential' discography of the Blomstedt and the SFSO which is likely the most important series of its kind in the late twentieth century (although it comprises but two complete symphony cycles, the Nielsen and Sibelius, top of their class, both), this disc is another fine testimony to this golden period in San Francisco, which I remember well.

Much would seem middle-of-the-road about these interpretations (tempi, overall timings), yet few unfamiliar with the maestro would expect the vitality of these unself-regarding interpretations, captured in superbly weighted and yet luminous sound. What would be the secret of Blomstedt and the SFSO? Agility, transparency, nuanced rhythmic impulse -- and complete engagement with Beethoven's world. Kudos to all for this exceptional legacy.

THIS IS NOT THE CHRISTMAS ALBUM YOU BELIEVE IT TO BE! If you're a fan of Fiona Ritchie's Thistle&Shamrock radio show on NPR like I am, you will probably approach this album with the same thought. That "it's going to be like one of her "Solstice" Christmas episodes" in December on her program. You would be COMPLETELY wrong. Those Solstice episodes are wonderful, and we stream them constantly during the Holiday season.
But what you have here, unbeknownst to me, is a "CEILIDH" Thistle and Shamrock Christmas. Ceilidh is described as "a traditional Gaelic social gathering, which usually involves playing Gaelic folk music and dancing". So what we have here are 17 recordings that sound like they fit perfectly in a tavern (on any night BUT one during Christmas week) with people clapping, stomping, dancing, and the whiskey freely flowing. This is great music to be sure - but to an American (6 generations removed from his Scottish ancestors) - this ISN'T CHRISTMAS MUSIC. Perhaps it is for those raised in Ireland. It is so extremely traditional that to those of us used to the Solstice program and raised in America, you won't even recognize anything resembling Christmas music. I love Fiona's weekly broadcasts, and couldn't wait to own this CD - so much so that I didn't bother to listen to the excerpts provided by Amazon. That was my mistake. And I paid $14.00 for that mistake. Read the reviews. I didn't.

A curious and not altogether compelling disc. C P E Bach's name is not shown on the cover, but his wonderful flute concerto in d minor is the highlight of this release. The first of the two J C Bach 'symphonies' Is about 11 minutes long (track 1 oddly doesn't begin until 0-24"), which is quite short for a 5-movement work, the clavier concerto reaches 13 minute mark, and the g minor 'Sturm und Drang' is 14 minutes long. Even more dubious than the shortness of the duration of these works is the lack of inspiration (the g minor just stops in the middle of nowhere).

The comments made by JC's older brother, cited in the liner notes, I'm afraid, are quite on the mark (J C's music had 'no heart').

Even with this tepid brew by J C, releasing a full price CD with 54' of music seems unacceptable. Performances and sonics are superb.

This a fortunate marvel of a recording.  Scherchen, despite his legacy of superb Beethoven and Haydn symphonies, was associated by many with lesser known, disfavored or contemporaneous composers. This is a disappointing disc but it's not altogether surprising. The further Kempff got away from the Austro-German tradition, the less satisfactory his playing is on an objective basis (his Chopin is equally well-intended and similarly awkward). The problems assert themselves in many areas but above all in the foursquare, literal interpretations at speeds which are comfortable for the octogenarian (Kempff was never in the top class of pianists technically). As a result, where is the poetry that interpretative insight adds to the literal rendition of notes? It's tiresome to be so aware of the pianist counting 4 and 8 bar measures -- even when Liszt deviates, and the lurches into piano/pianissimo are seldom convincing. There is no rhapsody here, no hint that Liszt was torn, his entire life, between the worldly and spiritual, and no sweep of passion that threatened to break the romantic mold. Even at slowish speeds, Kempff never brings the listener to the serene, still heart of these introspective masterpieces. The less said about the Saint Francois, the better.

That there are far more engaged accounts in the catalogue is clear (Arrau and Brendel are similar, that is, to be equally avoided), but I'd like to single out an AdP:Italie that is not particularly well-known, but is the work of a profound Lisztian: Tamás Vásáry. You will have difficulty recognizing the music as the same -- it moves on a higher plane, in every pianistic and interpretive way -- and you can sense how Liszt changed forever during his Wanderjahre.

Incidentally, I agree that Siepmann's notes are embarrassing, and the sound on the disc is mushy and over-reverberant even allowing for its age.

This fulfills a certain need for this repertoire on a single disc and everything apart from the singing is well above-average. You can't imagine how superficial these readings are until you've heard others, such as the BWV 170 in the classic recording on L'Oiseau-Lyre with Janet Baker -- this is music that combines passion and devotion and it requires great artistry to achieve this combination.

I have a few Chailly Bruckner symphonies -- all early, and quite acceptable as these are immature, however interesting, works.

The 6th is altogether a different matter as it is truly in the line of great Bruckner symphonies. Bruckner never felt the need to revise it and, in tribute to its special quality of having been written in blazing heat, called it "der Keckeste" -- the English translation would be 'the kickiest'. The composer knew. It is also the only mature Bruckner symphony to open without string tremelo. It is monumental, completely accessible (it's tiresome to hear otherwise, particularly in view of the fact that, actually, it's the 5th symphony that can be accused of impenetrability), and one of the composer's greatest. I would say that the boldest pages in all of the symphonies is the conclusion of the first movement, whose breathtaking modulations invoked in Sir Donald Tovey the image of being tossed upon 'Homeric seas'. For me, it's more spine-tingling and visionary than all the Brahms symphonies put together.

I have many fine versions of the 6th, including Klemperer, Sawallisch, Blomstedt, Celibidache. This is not one of them.

This is loud, fast, and clueless in general -- all exposed by excellent recording. To be avoided by Bruckner lovers and neophytes alike.

This doesn't catch fire -- perhaps it is better than the current competition, but against the great Handel opera recordings which exist in multiple fine renditions (Ariodante, for example) this set is --- dullish. I think the problem is primarily with Curtis who is consistently uninspired (slowing down at cadences, metronymic otherwise, never serving the wonderful abandon and fire in this score) and flaccid. The singers are unremarkable or slightly worse --- Simone Kermes, who was so wonderful in the recent Vivaldi Andromeda, sounds vocally uneven, especially thin and wavery on high; Davislim is sludgy and short-breathed, Prina reasonably expressive but seemingly small-voiced, Lemieux on placid autopilot, her voice like chocolate oozing inarticulately from a frozen yogurt machine. The less said about the Bertarido, rough-voiced, and unintegrated between the head and chest registers, the better. She ruined the DG Giulio Cesare, so why not reward her with another starring role. All in all, this is the weakest of Curtis's Handel recordings; his Deidemia and Rodrigo were more vital.

Perhaps part of the problem is that this is top drawer Handel, and Curtis isn't quite up in that league, unlike Jacobs or McCreesh or Leppard or....so many others. He's less self-conscious and awkward with the lesser, undiscovered operas.

This version leaves an easy path to the cup for the ideal cast of Fleming, Scholl, Blythe, etc. Actually, there are multiple possibilities --- I just know this is a recording that, after half a dozen listens, and many blind auditions for fellow musicians, fails to rise to the occasion.

It's doubtful that a large voice capable of singing fiorature with  strict 'a tempo' technical fireworks --- albeit with contrasting slower, 'expressive' sections --- is sufficient in itself to create a convincing Norma.  With 111 performances under her belt, at least this performance of Sutherland fails to convince me that she understands Norma's predicament and can relay in 'musical' (dare I say, 'artistic')terms.  Lurching between the turgid and rather graceless 'Casta Diva' to the bullet-like precision of "Bella a me ritorna" is not very eloquent (Bartoli does this sort of thing with greater flair).  With a conventionally 'uglier' voice, Callas is still to be preferred.  Perhaps Sills as well (haven't heard her, but she consistently understands what she's singing)...Deutekom in Vienna was more vital, while Caballe comes closest to the ideal combination of feeling, technique, and 'bella voce'.  All-in-all, uninteresting as art, except perhaps to fanciers of large canaries.

Painful as it was to listen to the whole disc, I did it if only to conclude that this was a serious misjudgment on all parts. While the concept is worthy, it utterly fails through the pianist's evident unfamiliarity with the mostly organ originals -- and a misguided, romantically overblown approach to these classics. If you can't play these technically demanding works 'a tempo' (with a consistent beat), then they should be off limits.

Either you like this approach, in which case Michelangeli and Horowitz were on the wrong track, or you revere the classic interpretive approach.

The final work on the disc, the unique Toccata, Adagio and Fugue in C (BWV 564) is shredded to tatters. Virtually every bar expresses ego rather than Bach. A simple comparison with the live Horowitz rendition which opened his return to Carnegie Hall in 1965 (a highly eccentric choice it was thought at the time) may shock the many reviewers here who have praised this misbegotten effort to the skies.

Kudos to Ms Mathé who was one of the first (apart from the composer himself) to record (1992) the complete solo piano works of this very great composer, who wrote nothing that was less than individual, deeply felt, and timeless in beauty. She doubtless captures the Innigkeit of this difficult but non-virtuosic music, as does Esther Flückiger for Nuova Era. Kathryn Stott displays sovereign technical address (in the 8 Preludes) in her fine recording for BIS which is coupled with the superb 'Cello Concerto with the excellent Christian Poltéra.

Sadly the negatives reviews here reflect a Manichean world where there are only one and five stars.

While one. might grumble that this edition is not needed, or is too similar to the virtuoso approaches of the Alban Berg or Emerson Quartets, there is ample evidence of some meaningful departures from the glossy, impersonal style that is currently the "international" standard of modernity and relevance (see the adagio of op18/6 or the opening movement of op131). Nonetheless, the serene stillness of the Veghs or Quartetto Italianos in the slow movements is clearly not the norm here. . But that was then, this is now. Certainly the most noteworthy cycle of recent years (sadly the Hagens and Petersens did not complete; and fine as they are the Auryn and Alexander versions, they are less representative I think of the younger generation). Were the Jerusalems to do a cycle, that would be the evident competition.  The Takacs are too stressed and even crude at times to please this listener consistently.

Having heard Serkin live in his 'prime' in NYC, and as well as the recordings from his later years, there's no doubt this is Serkin at his best.

Yet there remains at least 3 reasons why the audience at Lugano and myself (in virtual attendance) cannot really get fired about this recital.

First, the repertoire is largely the 'four-bar phrase' composers that Schnabel before, and Brendel afterwards, cultivated. It makes for a distinctly less interesting recital than Serkin's peers would have assembled (eg, Richter, Michelangeli, Rubinstein, Horowitz, etc). Apart from Bach, everything was composed in the half century following 1815, and by Austro-German composers. Excepting the Beethoven, none provides the least 'break' or countersubject for the prevailing musical tapestry of the time. Hence, the program is on the dull side.

Second, the greatest work programmed (the Appassionata) receives the least interesting rendition: dutiful but quite overpedalled (contrast Richter in the same period), with hardly any observance of the subtle textural notations (rests, staccati, sforzandi, etc) that Beethoven notated with precision. The mad scramble of the closing of the sonata is really Serkin thinking 'and now I have to go really fast, play loud, and bring out the PASSION!', so it's indifferently articulated digitally (meaning fingers), rhythmically unstable, and gelatinous in texture due to the overpedalling. The final chords are just electrical twitches in a gooey protoplasm of sound.

If a pianist really doesn't have anything individual to say about a composition, he or she should avoid it (as Richter and Michelangeli were careful to do). Are you listening, Murray? As for the Brahms, the various Michelangeli recordings are much more authoritative. Serkin had moments, like Arrau, when his technique produced ungainliness, a sound that was 180 degrees apart from the fluid, diaphonous recreations of Arturo Benedetti.

Third, perhaps most importantly, there scarcely a moment of surprise or striking perception. Alright, he never had the mental capacity of Richter, or occupied the crystalline sound world of Michelangeli, or had the incomparable romantic elan of Rubinstein -- or the succumbing, persuasive quirkiness of Horowitz. This is as close as Serkin got to playing in that league.

That leaves us with a musician and pedagogue who is greatly to be respected and honored, but not the unforgettable experience of a true interpretive genius. I suspect most of the rhapsodic reviews here have been written after ingesting the over-the-top comments by the usually perceptive Pierro Rattalino.

A very useful panorama of the breadth of Liszt's genius in lieder -- which far exceed imho those of contemporaries such as Brahms, if not Wolf. I go out in a limb to say that he ranks alongside Schumann, and that will be the eventual critical concensus. Very fine Hyperion production, including texts, translations and notes; superb piano collaboration by Julius Drake (not surprisingly).

Kirchschlager's basic timbre is quite beautiful in the middle of her vocal range but is colorless at the lower range (which is exceptionally important in this selection of songs) and tending to stridency and thinness at the top. Thus, the overwhelming drama of songs such as Der du Von den Himmel bist', 'Drei Zigeuner', 'Uber alle Gipfeln' is somewhat compromised by her current vocal estate. Perhaps recorded a little late in the singer's career.

Nonetheless, recommended. For the last word in great Liszt lieder interpretation, the standard remains the incomparable Janet Baker in an inexpensive but textless EMI duo --- further leveraged by an unforgettable Schumann Liederkreis (Op 39).

This is quite disappointing inasmuch as the Morike lieder are without any doubt the greatest lieder in the literature, equalled by not surpassed by certain Schubert, Schumann and Strauss works. But as a supreme collection of songs completed in some nine months, Wolf's works are without equal.

One of the earliest compact discs devoted to this absolute master, it remains unsurpassed despite recent, superb work by groups such as Huelgas and Brabant. It stands as a beacon for its premiere recording of Gombert’s single movement Credo, in my opinion the greatest composition of the first half of the sixteenth century.

The performances of Ars Nova themselves are transparent, with balanced weighting of registers (unlike Tallis with its alluring focus on the highest lines); the dozen voices have special timbral beauty, and are unerringly pitched. There is no interpretative layer to confound the essence of Gombert.

In short, this disc breathes the true spirit of Poulenc. The recording sounds lifelike and true, and the OPL plays superbly, including the violinist soloist, Philippe Koch. Everything needed to step away, however briefly, from the cares of the moment and be transported to Paris in the 40s.

This disc features a splendid soloist (Rostropovich's final pupil) playing an exquisite instrument (Cremona, late 17th C) in important works: the two authentic Haydn 'cello concerti (out of the 8 in the Hoboken catalogue), with a bonus of a contemporaneous concerto by Haydn's pupil Ignaz Pleyel.  Superbly balanced and transparent recording, with an appropriately plush but open sonic soundstage, fine notes, and cover art by Corot of a monk playing a cello -- a required recording for those who have interest in concerted works for the 'cello.

This disc presents one of the earliest live captures of this legend — her debut recital at the Edinburgh Festival in 1972. Although she was not yet 27, she strode like a colossus through the European opera houses, singing Elisabeth at Deutsche Oper at 24, and going on to make her La Scala and Covent Garden debuts in 1972. The rest is a grand history.

That she always deeply loved lieder was evident from the start. Here she sings Schubert, Brahms, Ravel, R Strauss with a Mahler encore with Irwin Gage accompanying her. The 68 minute recital is a very special part of the legacy of this great American singer.

Despite its historical importance, this review is written from the standpoint of whether this recording bears repeat listening in view of multiple alternatives, both historical/recent and live/studio. My conclusion is: no based on three basic factors. First, this score is heavily abridged (typical of its time certainly); second, there is only decent singing at best (despite its starry ledger) with much squeezed tone, wonky trills, dried rubber legatos, etc, and third, the sound is a serious detriment to this extraordinary score, perhaps Mozart’s most ambitious and wide-ranging dramatic score. The onstage “special” effects of clomping and thumping are overdone.

Despite strong leadership by Fricsay, the performance as a whole is for specialist interest only willing to endure the clotted, opaque sound and frequent ungainly singing (today’s “international” style of singing Mozart is of a vastly higher standard than in 1961). There are at least a half dozen recordings that are better library choices.

It’s difficult to imagine a more wonderful disc of chamber works of Reynaldo Hahn, close friend of Proust and composer of a range of works spanning opera, orchestral works and, notably, mélodies. His works are characterized by an unforced lyrical beauty, transparency of textures and a touch of melancholy, similar to that of so much of interwar French music. It’s difficult to contemplate Hindemith’s gnarly works of the same period, and though musicologists will always the palm to the Germanic school, I greatly admire and prefer the joyous and serene beauty of the French school.

Caballe at the absolute summum of her greatness, and the finer of her two live renditions. It remains not only the best recording of this wonderful work, but critical evidence that Caballe was the superb bel canto singer of her generation (pace Sutherland who was so often mechanical by comparison).

The comprehensive review previously posted is authoritative and I add a few personal observations. Due to the small size of the ensemble as has been noted (9 singers), the clarity of the parts are really extraordinary. There is also an intimacy that brings one closer to the music (both as pitches and rhythms, and expressive art) than, say, Tallis and A Sei Voce, remarkable as they are. Indeed, as observed before, there is really a kind of fearless confrontation by Metamorphoses with the composer which is less bloodless ('abstract' perhaps) than others. At times, there is true vigor and yet at other points the motive appears in the Josquin web of sound in quite a ghostly fashion.  The culminative effect is very different to Tallis -- in fact, I can hardly recognize these works in this rendering. I put this atop the similarly configured alternatives -- it would be challenged imho only if the Helgas/van Nevel were to record this (they've only rendered the wonderful Pipelare mass).

The Wolf, as has been noted, is [still] not available separately on CD.  This disc is the pinnacle of modern Wolf interpretation -- many sopranos have attempted Wolf with varying degrees of success, but few with the simplicity, subtlety and technique of Ameling -- and none with the unique pellucid tone (and the competition I've heard includes Schwarzkopf, Seefried, Hendricks, Cotrubas, Lott; only Auger is at this level of perfection).  This one disc, for lieder lovers, justifies the box -- with its own formidable delights.

As a Mozartian of subtlety and imagination, one would expect the transition to Beethoven to be reasonable. Unfortunately, as in the case of Angela Hewitt, the leap in musical, notational and rhetorical skills required by Beethoven is not met. A basically lyrical approach is not unreasonable, but it must recognize that it exists within a framework of very complex, highly notated and detailed scores, constantly marked with szforzandi, tempo fluctuations, phrasing indications, staccati, and so forth. The Uchida soup, bland as it is, is not very nourishing.

This cannot be a recommended version in light of so many great alternatives from the past (one reviewer has mentioned Schnabel and Richter, and many other marvelous readings by Serkin, Gilels, Kempff, etc ) as well as the present (Paul Lewis, Richard Goode).

A superb disc in every way: mostly rarely encountered but worthy repertoire; a generous program played brilliantly by Matthias Havinga (including a Vivace movement by a composer-forebearer); exceptional sonics, and very informative notes by the organist.  The modest price for the Brilliant release suggests an imperative purchase.

Superb conducting (equal to Bohm), very fine sound, and a great, great Nurse. Empress is a jugendliche soprano, very fine. Everyone else is forgettable: Muff hasn't any nobility as Barak with his colorless, underpowered voice; the Dyer's Wife has a crone's voice, with screeching on high notes to match; the Emperor is anything but a young sound, with wobbles so wide that time stands still (catch up on email). Even the tiny but essential role of the Falcon is entrusted to a soprano whose dessicated sounds will certainly strike fear in your heart but for the wrong reasons.

Pity the conductor, orchestra and Nurse & Empress --- why did they bother? As for EMI, is it any wonder they've withdrawn from classical recordings with judgements like this?

Incredibly beautiful sounding, lush and perfectly vocalized.  Unfortunately, it has little to do with the essence of this music, despite its accuracy and vast soundstage.  While it's not a truckdriver's interpretation, its interpretative value is modest, at best.

Wonderfully played and recorded, this disc showcases an agile, unmannered pianist who is the equal of any who have played this music, and certainly superior to (for example) the almost unlistenable Pletnev. Highly recommended alongside the classic Horowitz and more recent Christian Zacharias discs.

I didn't buy this album, but I listened to it from my friend's CD. This is just boring pop music. If you're looking for an Asian singer who's trying to break into the U.S. market by singing English, try Hikaru Utada.
Coco has the voice for Mandarin ballads/pop. Even people who don't understand Chinese rather listen to her Chinese songs. Her English songs are boring and lame....reminds me of Jessica Simpson's first album with those lame upbeat tracks (with the exception of "I wanna love you forever," "your faith in me," "heart of innocence," and "where you are.")
The music was great, but one of the discs is damaged and I could not play it through.

From the beginning, the wide stage acoustic, soloists scooping and straining, and the overall sheer heaviness, the conductor seems to think of this as a proto-Verdi Requiem. For the sympathetic, this might be a recording of choice but for me the bloat overwhelms. Although the work was written strictly on commission, something is awry here. Creed is preferable.

This is a fine Ring allowing for the vintage -- but the cream of the Bayreuth Rings on disc remains that of Clemens Krauss featuring many of the same singers (Varnay is certainly caught in finer voice in 53) -- the conducting is consistently more alert, the recording more transparent and true to life, and the vocalism fresher by all significant principal singers.
While you can't really compare cycles of this period with those of the modern stereo era, I certainly think a truer picture of the Ring is present here than in the synthetic Rings (such as Karajan's precious box of chocolates). In modern digital Rings, no one has displaced the Janowski in either sound or naturalness of approach.

A disappointing release as I’ve enjoyed Goodman in the past. These renditions lack the witty transparency that is embedded in the notes. Instead, we have a ‘big band’ sound which (at least here) doesn’t convince (whereas Scherchen’s did).  The famous fortissimo chord in the slow movement of the 94th is ‘surprising’ in the wrong kind of way (like a pet accident). Most alternatives to Goodman will be superior, ranging from the classic (Scherchen, Dorati, Szell) to original instruments (Koopman, Fischer) — lots of choices.
Not enough of what I exoected on this CD.
Forgettable.  Appreciate the effort, but no tunes that I would ever want to hear again.

I am not a professional product rater such as appears on Amazon so much (are they paid by companies to do this?), just a regular person.  As such I am not going to tell you every little detail about this compilation, one because I don't have the time and 2 because why don't you just listen to it.  But I will say this has been one of the more enjoyable purchases of music i have made in the last few years and if you are a music lover and like this type of music you won't go wrong buying this.

A countervailing opinion here. Not interested in getting into an Aspergers-rooted discussion of 'could have, should have sung...' or 'A + B + C would have been ideal...'

This is the real world and we are fortunate to have this splendid document of wonderful music & conducting, and the very moving and exquisite singing of Caballe and the youthful Carreras. The group of six is rounded out by the excellent Manuguerra, Giaiotti, a distinctive Patricia Payne and a stunning Laura by Maria Luisa Nave. Let those who would conjure up the ghosts of great singing of the past go to their room. This is live, this was a recent 'today' (1979) and the sound is beyond reproach.

Let me also add that I find it mirthful that the Met should choose to mount such silliness as their Enchanted Island pasticcio or the most recent ephemera, the Tempest -- over this timeless verismo music. Two productions in 23 years, thanks, Met. At least we know what beds Peter Gelb lies in.

We can fully expect him to announce a Met premiere of Mozart's La finta gardiniera as touched up by Turnage, with sets by Hirst and, in a first, costumes by Beckham with the inimitable Natalie Dessay singing all the soprano roles.

I will be brief largely because of my disappointed in the sound quality of these recordings. I have downloaded various sounds of nature and the quality is crisp and clear. This CD actually is horrible. I even thought maybe it is the new music I bought from amazon that is problem but I went to my desktop that has Bose speakers and the sound was horrible too. So I am returning this one and the other one I also plan to review.
I really wanted this to work out because I love the sound of the ocean--it has always served as a calming effect for me but with this recording calm and relaxed is not the experience.

Fortunately the Haydn quartets, even the comparatively modest Op 17, can benefit from differing interpretive stances. The Festetics see this set as an early pre-mature (ie pre-Op 20) opus, and approach it with little hint of the Papa-Haydnish conventional 'warmth' to come. The simplicity and straightforwardness of their stance is fully convincing. True, there are occasional intonational problems, suggesting an off day or insufficient practice (happily free from the other opus in their cycle) but part of this naturally arises from the wiry and revealing sound of original instrument gut strings. Still, much recommended with its elevated packing and superior liner notes.

Wonderfully transparent and refined, yet with bustling energy and well-characterised rhythms -- this is my favorite Petrushka of 3 that I own (including the conductor's earlier version).  On top of it, the Detroit Symphony is exceptionally virtuosic and is recorded with an exceptionally wide soundstage -- demonstration class in short.  And, on top of that, the conductor's personal edition (unpublished) of an amalgam of the 1911/1947 versions of the score, with Stravinsky's implicit approval -- this is no ordinary 'very fine' Petrushka! It's in a class of its own.

Sad to report but a feeble attempt all around which proves along the way that Mozart is not Beethoven and a major recording contract is not proof of understanding.

The reviewer from Norway has it exactly right, but is really too kind. This disc is about the notes that the composer wrote, not the music and world view of Beethoven. You can barely guess that the Hammerklavier is a kind of summum of piano literature -- Uchida is entirely too bland and characterless to be considered anywhere near a great reading. The clarity of the recording is cruel indeed.

For the distant past, listen to Schnabel (clearly a fallible technique but such a profound thinking musician recorded that muffled sonics cannot dim), for the recent past, Richter; and for a lighter, aristocratic reading that may be what Uchida was attempting to achieve, the pellucid Richard Goode is to be greatly preferred.
I can't really review this because it won't play.
This is good cd, but does have a few filler songs. Personally I'm a tired of Ozzy, his act has gotten a little old for me.

An off-night for Caballe who reveals blowsiness and stressful singing which may be traceable to Verrett who is unrelentingly aggressive and unmusical. On that occasion in Milan, Caballe apparently scolded Verrett (as she deserved for her coarse singing) and thus this recording is a failure.  Fortunately (on the same label) is a far superior Caballe Maria Stuarda with Carreras as Leicester, no less, and a far more cogent Elizabeth.
